brief_justification: Wrong answer (Yes vs. correct No), though the causal-chain logic is not entirely incoherent; Only surface-level causal chain stated — no exploration of moral responsibility vs. physical causation, counterfactuals, or how 'typical person' intuitions about helpful intermediaries work; No questioning of own framing, no pause to consider whether 'typical person' intuition might run opposite to the stated conclusion; No counterfactual check, no boundary-condition test (e.g., 'would people blame a good samaritan?'), no sanity check against the correct answer direction; Conclusion stated with confidence, no acknowledgment that causal attribution in intervening-actor scenarios is contested or that the 'helpful neighbor' framing commonly shifts intuitions; Drunk driver is mentioned as 'also a cause' but the agent never seriously evaluates why a typical person might exonerate the neighbor (he was helping, not creating risk), leaving the No answer unexplored; Causal chain is shown (neighbor drove → car hit → son hurt) but the derivation of why this reflects 'typical person' reasoning — rather than expert legal/philosophical causation — is asserted without grounding.
